title: Strohhalm, Kohle und Bohne
titleEn: The Straw, the Coal, and the Bean
part: The Straw, the Coal, and the Bean
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 18. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 18, one of the Grimms’ short comic origin-tales. A straw, a coal, and a bean each escape an old woman’s cooking fire and set off together; crossing a brook on the straw, the coal burns it through and both perish, while the bean laughs so hard it bursts and is stitched up by a passing tailor — which is why, ever since, every bean carries a black seam. The register is dry and mock-heroic, the objects making speeches like escaped soldiers. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as fieng, Rath, Theil, gewiß, sechszig, and the archaic von wannen (whence).

sentences.t: In einem Dorfe wohnte eine arme alte Frau, die hatte ein Gericht Bohnen zusammen gebracht und wollte sie kochen.
sentences.l: In a village lived a poor old woman, who had brought together a dish of beans and wanted to cook them.
sentences.i: In a village there lived a poor old woman who had gathered a dish of beans and meant to cook them.
sentences.n: „ein Gericht“ here means a dish or serving of food (not a court of law). „zusammenbringen“ (split: zusammen … gebracht) = to bring together, gather up. The relative „die hatte“ points back to the woman with a demonstrative — a common Grimm construction.

sentences.t: Sie machte also auf ihrem Herd ein Feuer zurecht, und damit es desto schneller brennen sollte, zündete sie es mit einer Hand voll Stroh an.
sentences.l: So she made ready a fire on her hearth, and so that it should burn all the faster, she lit it with a handful of straw.
sentences.i: So she got a fire ready on her hearth, and to make it burn all the quicker, she kindled it with a handful of straw.
sentences.n: „zurechtmachen“ (split: machte … zurecht) = to make ready, prepare; „anzünden“ (split: zündete … an) = to light, kindle — both separable, the prefix flung to the end of the clause. „desto schneller“ = all the faster; „desto“ + comparative marks a corresponding degree.

sentences.t: Als sie die Bohnen in den Topf schüttete, entfiel ihr unbemerkt eine, die auf dem Boden neben einen Strohhalm zu liegen kam; bald danach sprang auch eine glühende Kohle vom Herd zu den beiden herab.
sentences.l: As she poured the beans into the pot, one slipped from her unnoticed, which came to lie on the floor beside a straw; soon after, a glowing coal too sprang down from the hearth to the two of them.
sentences.i: As she was pouring the beans into the pot, one slipped from her unnoticed and came to rest on the floor beside a straw; soon after, a glowing coal likewise leapt down from the hearth to join the pair.
sentences.n: „entfallen“ (here entfiel) = to fall out of, slip from — with the dative „ihr“ (from her). „zu liegen kommen“ = to come to lie, end up lying. „herab“ = down; it pairs with sprang to give „sprang … herab,“ leapt down.

sentences.t: Da fieng der Strohhalm an und sprach „liebe Freunde, von wannen kommt ihr her?“
sentences.l: Then the straw began and spoke: “dear friends, from whence do you come here?”
sentences.i: Then the straw spoke up and said: “Dear friends, whence do you come?”
sentences.n: „anfangen“ (split: fieng … an) = to begin; „fieng“ is the 1857 spelling of modern „fing.“ „von wannen“ is an archaic „whence, from where“ (modern „woher“); „her“ reinforces motion toward the speaker.

sentences.t: Die Kohle antwortete „ich bin zu gutem Glück dem Feuer entsprungen, und hätte ich das nicht mit Gewalt durchgesetzt, so war mir der Tod gewiß: ich wäre zu Asche verbrannt.“
sentences.l: The coal answered: “I have, by good luck, sprung away from the fire, and had I not forced that through by main strength, then death was certain for me: I would have burned to ashes.”
sentences.i: The coal answered: “By good luck I escaped the fire, and had I not forced my way out by main strength, death would have been certain for me — I'd have burned to ashes.”
sentences.n: „dem Feuer entsprungen“ — „entspringen“ (to escape, spring away) takes a dative object (dem Feuer). „hätte ich … durchgesetzt“ is a conditional with „wenn“ dropped: fronting the verb (had I not…) replaces „wenn.“ „zu gutem Glück“ = luckily; you'll see it again.

sentences.t: Die Bohne sagte „ich bin auch noch mit heiler Haut davon gekommen, aber hätte mich die Alte in den Topf gebracht, ich wäre ohne Barmherzigkeit zu Brei gekocht worden, wie meine Kameraden.“
sentences.l: The bean said: “I too got away with whole skin, but had the old woman brought me into the pot, I would without mercy have been cooked to mush, like my comrades.”
sentences.i: The bean said: “I too came off with a whole skin, but had the old woman got me into the pot, I'd have been boiled to mush without mercy, like my comrades.”
sentences.n: „mit heiler Haut davonkommen“ = to escape unharmed, get off with a whole skin (idiom). „die Alte“ = the old woman (a nominalized adjective). „wäre … gekocht worden“ is a past conditional passive: „worden“ (not „geworden“) marks the passive.

sentences.t: „Wäre mir denn ein besser Schicksal zu Theil geworden?“ sprach das Stroh, „alle meine Brüder hat die Alte in Feuer und Rauch aufgehen lassen, sechszig hat sie auf einmal gepackt und ums Leben gebracht.
sentences.l: “Would a better fate then have fallen to my share?” spoke the straw, “all my brothers the old woman has let go up in fire and smoke, sixty she seized at once and did to death.
sentences.i: “And would a better fate have fallen to me?” said the straw. “The old woman let all my brothers go up in fire and smoke; sixty she grabbed at one stroke and put to death.
sentences.n: „jemandem zu Theil werden“ = to fall to someone's lot, be granted to (Theil is the 1857 spelling of Teil). „aufgehen lassen“ — „aufgehen“ here = to be consumed, go up (in flames). „ums Leben bringen“ = to kill; „sechszig“ is the 1857 spelling of sechzig.

sentences.t: Glücklicherweise bin ich ihr zwischen den Fingern durchgeschlüpft.“
sentences.l: Fortunately I slipped through, between her fingers.”
sentences.i: Luckily I slipped through her fingers.”
sentences.n: „durchschlüpfen“ (here durchgeschlüpft) = to slip through. The dative „ihr“ is a dative of possession — „through her fingers“ rather than a separate „her.“

sentences.t: „Was sollen wir aber nun anfangen?“ sprach die Kohle.
sentences.l: “But what are we to begin now?” spoke the coal.
sentences.i: “But what are we to do now?” said the coal.
sentences.n: „anfangen“ here in the sense „to do, set about“ (was sollen wir anfangen = what are we to do).

sentences.t: „Ich meine,“ antwortete die Bohne, „weil wir so glücklich dem Tode entronnen sind, so wollen wir uns als gute Gesellen zusammen halten und, damit uns hier nicht wieder ein neues Unglück ereilt, gemeinschaftlich auswandern und in ein fremdes Land ziehen.“
sentences.l: “I think,” answered the bean, “because we have so luckily escaped death, so let us hold together as good companions and, so that no new misfortune overtakes us here again, emigrate in common and move off into a foreign land.”
sentences.i: “I say,” answered the bean, “since we've so luckily escaped death, let us stick together as good companions and — so that no fresh misfortune overtakes us here again — emigrate together and set off for a foreign land.”
sentences.n: „dem Tode entrinnen“ = to escape death (dative object; „Tode“ has the old dative -e). „ereilen“ = to overtake, catch up with (of misfortune). „gemeinschaftlich“ = jointly, in common; „auswandern“ (separable) = to emigrate.

sentences.t: Der Vorschlag gefiel den beiden andern, und sie machten sich miteinander auf den Weg.
sentences.l: The proposal pleased the two others, and they set out together on the way.
sentences.i: The proposal pleased the other two, and they set off together.
sentences.n: „gefallen“ = to please, be pleasing (with dative: den beiden andern). „sich auf den Weg machen“ = to set out, get going (idiom).

sentences.t: Bald aber kamen sie an einen kleinen Bach, und da keine Brücke oder Steg da war, so wußten sie nicht wie sie hinüber kommen sollten.
sentences.l: But soon they came to a little brook, and as no bridge or footbridge was there, so they did not know how they should get across.
sentences.i: But soon they came to a little brook, and since there was no bridge or footbridge, they did not know how they were to get across.
sentences.n: „Steg“ = a narrow footbridge, plank walk. „hinüberkommen“ (separable, hinüber … kommen) = to get across, to the other side.

sentences.t: Der Strohhalm fand guten Rath und sprach „ich will mich quer über legen, so könnt ihr auf mir wie auf einer Brücke hinüber gehen.“
sentences.l: The straw found good counsel and spoke: “I will lay myself crosswise over, so you can go across on me as on a bridge.”
sentences.i: The straw hit on a good plan and said: “I'll lay myself crosswise over, and then you can walk across on me as on a bridge.”
sentences.n: „Rath“ is the 1857 spelling of Rat (counsel); „guten Rath finden“ = to find a good solution. „quer über“ = crosswise, across; „hinübergehen“ = to walk over/across.

sentences.t: Der Strohhalm streckte sich also von einem Ufer zum andern, und die Kohle, die von hitziger Natur war, trippelte auch ganz keck auf die neugebaute Brücke.
sentences.l: So the straw stretched itself from one bank to the other, and the coal, which was of hot nature, tripped quite boldly onto the newly-built bridge.
sentences.i: So the straw stretched itself from one bank to the other, and the coal, being of a fiery nature, tripped quite boldly onto the newly-built bridge.
sentences.n: „hitziger Natur“ = of a hot, fiery temperament — a wry pun, since the coal is literally hot. „trippeln“ = to trip, patter with quick little steps; „keck“ = bold, saucy.

sentences.t: Als sie aber in die Mitte gekommen war und unter ihr das Wasser rauschen hörte, ward ihr doch angst: sie blieb stehen und getraute sich nicht weiter.
sentences.l: But when it had come into the middle and heard the water rushing beneath it, it grew afraid after all: it stopped still and did not dare go further.
sentences.i: But when it had reached the middle and heard the water rushing beneath it, it grew frightened after all: it halted and dared go no further.
sentences.n: „ward“ is the old past of „werden“ (modern „wurde“); „ihr wird angst“ = one becomes afraid — an impersonal dative construction (literally „to her it grew fearful“). „stehen bleiben“ = to stop, stand still; „sich getrauen“ = to dare.

sentences.t: Der Strohhalm aber fieng an zu brennen, zerbrach in zwei Stücke und fiel in den Bach: die Kohle rutschte nach, zischte wie sie ins Wasser kam und gab den Geist auf.
sentences.l: But the straw began to burn, broke into two pieces and fell into the brook: the coal slid after, hissed as it came into the water and gave up the ghost.
sentences.i: But the straw caught fire, broke in two, and fell into the brook; the coal slid after it, hissed as it hit the water, and gave up the ghost.
sentences.n: „nachrutschen“ (split: rutschte … nach) = to slide after. „den Geist aufgeben“ = to give up the ghost, die (idiom) — grimly apt for a coal going out in the water.

sentences.t: Die Bohne, die vorsichtigerweise noch auf dem Ufer zurückgeblieben war, mußte über die Geschichte lachen, konnte nicht aufhören und lachte so gewaltig daß sie zerplatzte.
sentences.l: The bean, which cautiously had still stayed behind on the bank, had to laugh over the affair, could not stop and laughed so mightily that it burst apart.
sentences.i: The bean, which had cautiously stayed behind on the bank, could not help laughing at the whole affair, could not stop, and laughed so hard that it burst.
sentences.n: „zurückbleiben“ (separable) = to stay behind. „über etwas lachen müssen“ = to be unable to help laughing at something. „Geschichte“ here = the affair, the business (not simply „story“). „zerplatzen“ = to burst apart.

sentences.t: Nun war es ebenfalls um sie geschehen, wenn nicht zu gutem Glück ein Schneider, der auf der Wanderschaft war, sich an dem Bach ausgeruht hätte.
sentences.l: Now it would likewise have been all over with it, if not, by good luck, a tailor who was on his travels had rested himself by the brook.
sentences.i: Now it would have been all up with it too, had not a tailor, who happened to be on his travels, luckily been resting by the brook.
sentences.n: „es ist um jemanden geschehen“ = it is all over with someone, they are done for (idiom). „auf der Wanderschaft“ = on one's travels — the journeyman's custom of wandering town to town to learn the trade. „sich ausruhen“ = to rest oneself.

sentences.t: Weil er ein mitleidiges Herz hatte, so holte er Nadel und Zwirn heraus und nähte sie zusammen.
sentences.l: Because he had a compassionate heart, so he took out needle and thread and sewed it together.
sentences.i: Because he had a compassionate heart, he took out needle and thread and sewed it up.
sentences.n: „Zwirn“ = twisted sewing-thread. „herausholen“ (split: holte … heraus) = to take out; „zusammennähen“ (split: nähte … zusammen) = to sew together.

sentences.t: Die Bohne bedankte sich bei ihm aufs schönste, aber da er schwarzen Zwirn gebraucht hatte, so haben seit der Zeit alle Bohnen eine schwarze Naht.
sentences.l: The bean thanked him most beautifully, but because he had used black thread, so since that time all beans have a black seam.
sentences.i: The bean thanked him most warmly, but because he had used black thread, ever since then all beans have a black seam.
sentences.n: The etiological punch line: the whole tale exists to explain why beans have a dark seam down their side. „sich bedanken“ = to give thanks (reflexive; sich bei jm. bedanken). „aufs schönste“ = most warmly, in the finest way (aufs + superlative). „gebrauchen“ = to use.
